items.headline: U.S. Stocks Conclude Fourth Consecutive Losing Week Amid Geopolitical Tensions and Surging Oil Prices
items.summary: U.S. equity markets ended Friday, March 20, 2026, lower, marking the fourth straight week of declines for the S&P 500. Investor sentiment was primarily weighed down by escalating Middle East tensions and a significant surge in crude oil prices, which fueled inflation concerns and led to defensive positioning. Major indices closed notably down, with technology and growth stocks facing broad selling pressure.

items.headline: SolarEdge Technologies
items.summary: Topped the list of performers among US-listed stocks covered by Morningstar analysts.

items.headline: ALEANNA INC
items.summary: Showed positive momentum during Friday's session.

items.headline: TORRID HOLDINGS INC
items.summary: Investors celebrated after the company reported fourth quarter and fiscal 2025 results and initiated fiscal 2026 guidance.

items.headline: U Power Limited
items.summary: Experienced panic selling following an announced and priced $6 million public offering of units, which was heavily dilutive.

items.headline: Reviva Pharmaceuticals Holdings, Inc.
items.summary: Fell significantly after pricing a $10 million public offering of shares plus warrants at a discount, following recent weakness and a reverse split.

items.headline: Super Micro Computer Inc.
items.summary: Shares decreased following a press release about a fraud investigation. This also raised concerns about export controls and supply chain risk across the AI sector.

items.headline: Energy Sector Leads as Utilities and Tech Lag
items.summary: The energy sector was the best-performing sector for the week ending March 20, rising roughly 3%, driven by surging oil prices. In contrast, sectors like utilities, basic materials, software, real estate, technology, and consumer discretionary were among the worst performers. This rotation into energy highlights the market's reaction to the ongoing Middle East conflict and its impact on commodities.

items.headline: Federal Reserve Holds Rates Steady Amid Geopolitical Uncertainty
items.summary: The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) decided to keep the federal funds rate target range unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% at its March 18, 2026 meeting. There was one dissenting vote in favor of a cut. The FOMC's Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) showed upward revisions to inflation and growth forecasts for 2026 and 2027. Chair Powell emphasized data dependence and the 'uncertain' implications of Middle East developments on the U.S. economy, especially concerning oil prices.

items.headline: Geopolitical Tensions Drive Oil Prices Higher and Fuel Inflation Concerns
items.summary: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East, including the Iranian blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, has led to a significant surge in oil prices, with Brent crude moving above $112 and WTI near $97.91 per barrel. This has amplified headline inflation pressures, leading traders to speculate that the Federal Reserve may be unable to cut rates this year. The uncertainty surrounding the conflict continues to shape market sentiment and macroeconomic outlooks globally.

items.headline: Labor Market Shows Softening Amid Mixed Economic Data
items.summary: Recent economic data has been mixed, with job growth accelerating in January but reversing sharply in February, resulting in 92,000 net jobs lost. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in February. This softening in the labor market, combined with rising inflation from higher oil prices, presents a challenging balancing act for the Federal Reserve.
